clean_story:
White light fractured. Rainbows from glass.

The word prism comes from Greek prisma—“something sawed,” because early prisms were cut glass wedges that sliced light like a knife through layered cake.

Sunlight seems colorless, but it’s a crowded highway of red, blue, and every shade between, all racing together. When this mixed beam hits a prism’s angled face, each color slows at a slightly different speed—like runners hitting deep sand after a sprint. Violet stumbles the most, bending sharply; red barely falters. The colors fan out, exposing their hidden separation. This isn’t magic: it’s precision. A 60-degree glass prism spreads sunlight into a spectrum 40 times wider than the original beam.

Isaac Newton proved this in 1666 by drilling a hole in his shutter, letting sunlight strike a prism, then catching the rainbow on a wall. He reassembled the colors with a lens, turning them back into white—a test no one had tried before. Today, prisms sort light in telescopes, fiber optics, and even grocery scanners, where they split laser beams to read barcodes.

Three related subjects are color as physics, lens versus prism, and how eyes deceive.
